title: What Is Spot Price?
meta_title: What Is Spot Price? Precious Metals Wiki
meta_description: Plain-language explanation of precious-metal spot price and why physical coins and bars usually trade above or below it.
summary: Spot price is the quoted market price for a precious metal, usually expressed per troy ounce. It is a reference point, not the exact price a consumer pays or receives for a physical coin or bar.

sections.heading: Spot is a benchmark
sections.body: The spot price gives buyers and sellers a common reference for gold, silver, platinum, or palladium. Physical products still include premiums, spreads, fabrication costs, and dealer margins.

sections.heading: Physical products are not spot itself
sections.body: A one-ounce coin may contain one troy ounce of metal, but its market price can sit above spot because of minting, recognition, scarcity, and retail demand. A dealer buyback offer may sit below retail because the dealer needs a resale margin.

sections.heading: How to use spot responsibly
sections.body: Use spot as the baseline for melt value, then compare the actual product premium and the likely buyback spread. This helps prevent overpaying when buying and underselling when selling.

disclaimer: This article is educational and does not provide live pricing, investment advice, or guaranteed resale values.

title: Melt Value Explained
meta_title: Melt Value Explained for Coins and Bullion
meta_description: Learn how melt value is calculated for gold and silver coins, bars, and constitutional silver.
summary: Melt value is the estimated value of the precious metal content inside a coin, round, or bar. It is calculated from fine metal weight multiplied by the current spot price.

sections.heading: The basic formula
sections.body: Melt value equals fine precious-metal weight multiplied by spot price per troy ounce. For a 1 oz gold bullion coin, one troy ounce times gold spot gives the baseline metal value.

sections.heading: Coins can trade above melt
sections.body: Many coins trade above melt because they carry a premium for recognition, condition, scarcity, or mint origin. This is why melt value should be treated as a floor reference, not always the final market value.

sections.heading: Why sellers should calculate it
sections.body: Knowing melt value helps a seller evaluate whether an offer is fair, especially for common bullion, scrap, and constitutional silver.

disclaimer: Melt calculations are educational estimates and should be checked against current market quotes before a transaction.

title: Premiums Over Spot
meta_title: Premiums Over Spot for Gold and Silver
meta_description: Understand why coins, bars, and rounds trade above spot and how premiums affect buying and selling precious metals.
summary: A premium over spot is the amount paid above the metal's baseline spot value. Premiums vary by product type, mint recognition, size, demand, and dealer inventory.

sections.heading: Why premiums exist
sections.body: Physical products must be refined, fabricated, transported, inventoried, authenticated, and resold. Those costs and the market's demand for recognizable products create premiums.

sections.heading: Premiums differ by format
sections.body: Fractional gold often has higher percentage premiums than one-ounce gold. Sovereign coins often carry higher premiums than generic bars. Silver coins can carry much higher percentage premiums than large silver bars.

sections.heading: Premium does not equal profit
sections.body: A buyer may pay a premium at retail, but a future buyback offer may include a spread. The premium must be evaluated against liquidity and resale demand.

disclaimer: Premium examples are educational and vary by dealer, market conditions, and product availability.

title: Dealer Buyback Spread
meta_description: Learn why dealer buyback offers differ from retail prices and how to compare offers for coins, bars, and silver.
summary: The buyback spread is the difference between the price a dealer sells a product for and the price they are willing to pay to buy it back. Understanding the spread helps sellers compare offers more calmly.

sections.heading: Retail and buyback are different markets
sections.body: Retail pricing includes a dealer's cost of inventory, authentication, overhead, and resale margin. Buyback pricing reflects what the dealer can pay while still managing risk and reselling the item.

sections.heading: Highly liquid products usually have tighter spreads
sections.body: Major sovereign coins and recognized bars often have better buyback treatment than obscure products. Damaged, questionable, or unpackaged items may receive wider spreads.

sections.heading: How sellers can compare offers
sections.body: Ask whether the offer is based on spot, melt value, product premium, or numismatic value. Comparing two or three quotes can reveal whether one offer is unusually low.

disclaimer: Buyback spreads vary by dealer and market conditions. This page is educational, not a quote or recommendation.

title: Assay and Authentication
meta_title: Assay and Authentication for Precious Metals
meta_description: Reference guide to assay, weight, dimensions, magnet testing, and authentication considerations for precious-metal products.
summary: Authentication is the process of confirming that a coin, bar, or round is genuine and contains the expected metal content. No single casual test is perfect, especially for high-value gold.

sections.heading: Specifications matter
sections.body: Weight, diameter, thickness, edge detail, and design features should be checked against known specifications. A mismatch does not always prove fraud, but it requires caution.

sections.heading: Assay is stronger than appearance
sections.body: Gold-plated counterfeits and altered pieces can look convincing. Electronic testing, XRF, ultrasound, or professional assay can provide stronger evidence than visual inspection.

sections.heading: Packaging is helpful but not final
sections.body: Sealed assay packaging can support confidence for bars, but packaging can also be imitated. Trusted sources and careful verification remain important.

disclaimer: Authentication guidance is educational. For valuable items, use a qualified dealer, grading service, or professional testing method.

title: Liquidity and Divisibility
meta_title: Liquidity and Divisibility in Precious Metals
meta_description: Learn how product size affects flexibility, resale options, and portfolio divisibility for gold and silver.
summary: Liquidity describes how easily an asset can be sold. Divisibility describes how easily value can be broken into smaller pieces without selling more metal than intended.

sections.heading: Small products are flexible
sections.body: Fractional coins and constitutional silver can be easier to sell in smaller amounts. The tradeoff is that small products often carry higher percentage premiums when purchased.

sections.heading: Large products can be efficient
sections.body: Large bars may have lower premiums per ounce, but selling them requires finding a buyer for the full unit. That can reduce flexibility for a consumer seller.

sections.heading: A balanced stack considers both
sections.body: A practical metals position may include highly recognizable coins, divisible silver, and lower-premium bars depending on goals and resale expectations.

disclaimer: This page explains product structure and does not recommend a specific investment allocation.
